text: The article explores the rapid growth of the global trade in education as a commodity using the Australian example. The commodity form of education has become a global phenomenon. Since the introduction of the General Agreement on Trade in Services (GATS) the commodity form of higher education has become widely established. There are examples of many countries where education has become a major export, such as Australia. Education is the major contributor to the Australian export market. The commercialisation of international students in Australia is accompanied by unprecedented levels of public underfunding of higher education in Australia. Australia's success in education is built on foundations that are unstable and potentially volatile. The sustainability of Australia's quality of education, including student numbers in each class, as well as adequate funding for research and infrastructure of higher education, is threatened if Australian students continue to subsidise the international students studying in Australia. Further, the teaching of international students in Australian universities is not always undertaken in a way that complements high quality pedagogy for domestic students, because the former is seen as a cash cow to augment the latter. Therefore, Australia's current high level of participation in the global market for education raises serious longer term issues. These issues make Australia potentially hostage to a dangerous dependence on the volatile international education commodity markets. An alternative possibility is public reform.
